evals.prompt: Can you do a full security audit of our WooCommerce site? We're running WordPress 6.4.2 with about 30 plugins. URL: https://example.com
evals.expected_output: Should check for product-marketing-context.md first. Should ask about hosting environment, WP-CLI access, and existing security plugins. Should systematically cover all 10 audit categories: core/dependency updates, authentication, file system, database, HTTP headers, SSL, information disclosure, plugin/theme security, backup, and malware/integrity. Should check publicly accessible indicators via web_fetch (headers, version exposure, REST API enumeration, xmlrpc.php). Should produce a health score (0-100), findings with Issue/Severity/Impact/Evidence/Fix/Priority, a dependency report table, and a prioritized action plan.
assertions: Checks for product-marketing-context.md
Asks about hosting environment and WP-CLI access
Checks HTTP response headers for security headers and version disclosure
Checks for WordPress version exposure (meta generator, readme.html, RSS feed)
Checks REST API user enumeration endpoint
Checks xmlrpc.php accessibility
Covers dependency updates (core, plugins, themes, PHP version)
Covers authentication checks (admin username, 2FA, brute force protection)
Covers file system security (permissions, debug mode, file editor)
Covers HTTP security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options)
Covers SSL/HTTPS configuration
Produces a health score between 0 and 100
Each finding has Issue, Severity, Impact, Evidence, Fix, and Priority fields
Output includes a dependency report table
Output includes a prioritized action plan grouped by severity

evals.prompt: I think my WordPress site has been hacked. I'm seeing weird redirects to spam sites and there are some PHP files I don't recognize. URL: https://example.com
evals.expected_output: Should recognize this as an active incident and skip normal intake. Should prioritize malware and integrity checks first: core file verification, suspicious PHP files, recently modified files, hidden admin accounts, unauthorized cron jobs, .htaccess redirect rules, base64/eval patterns in theme files. Should recommend immediate containment steps (change passwords, revoke sessions, take site offline if needed). Should then proceed with a broader audit to identify the attack vector.
assertions: Recognizes this as an active security incident
Prioritizes malware and integrity checks before other audit categories
Recommends running wp core verify-checksums or equivalent
Checks for suspicious PHP files in web root and uploads
Checks for base64-encoded or obfuscated code
Checks for unauthorized admin accounts
Checks for unauthorized cron jobs
Checks .htaccess for suspicious redirect rules
Recommends immediate containment steps (password changes, session revocation)
After containment, investigates the attack vector

evals.prompt: Can you check if any of my WordPress plugins need updating? I have about 20 plugins installed.
evals.expected_output: Should recognize this as a narrow-scope request focused on dependency updates. Should not force a full security audit. Should ask for WP-CLI access or the site URL. Should check plugin versions against latest available, flag abandoned plugins (2+ years since last update), check for known vulnerabilities in installed versions, and produce a dependency report table. May suggest a broader security audit as a follow-up but should not insist.
assertions: Focuses on dependency audit without forcing a full security audit
Asks about WP-CLI access or requests plugin list
Checks plugin versions against latest available
Flags plugins that are outdated
Flags plugins that appear abandoned (2+ years since last update)
Mentions checking for known vulnerabilities in installed versions
Produces a dependency report table
Does not require the user to answer questions about all 10 audit categories

evals.prompt: I just set up a fresh WordPress install on my VPS. What should I do to secure it before launch?
evals.expected_output: Should recognize this as a proactive hardening request for a new installation. Should cover wp-config.php constants to set (DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT, FORCE_SSL_ADMIN, WP_DEBUG false, etc.), file permissions, security headers, authentication hardening (change admin username, enable 2FA, install security plugin), database prefix, XML-RPC disabling, directory listing, PHP execution in uploads, and backup setup. Should reference wp-hardening.md and security-headers.md. Should not check for malware or compromised files since it's a fresh install.
assertions: Recognizes this is a new install hardening request
Covers wp-config.php security constants to set
Covers file permission recommendations
Covers HTTP security headers to add
Recommends changing default admin username
Recommends enabling two-factor authentication
Recommends installing a security plugin
Recommends disabling XML-RPC if not needed
Recommends blocking PHP execution in uploads directory
Recommends setting up automated backups
Does not check for malware on a fresh install

evals.prompt: Can you check the security headers on my WordPress site? URL: https://example.com
evals.expected_output: Should recognize this as a narrow-scope request focused on HTTP security headers. Should fetch response headers via curl or web_fetch. Should check all 7 security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, X-XSS-Protection) against recommended values. Should also check for information-leaking headers (X-Powered-By, Server version). Should provide specific fix instructions with code for the user's server type (Apache/Nginx) or as a PHP mu-plugin. Should reference security-headers.md.
assertions: Focuses on headers without forcing a full security audit
Fetches HTTP response headers from the provided URL
Checks for Strict-Transport-Security header
Checks for Content-Security-Policy header
Checks for X-Content-Type-Options header
Checks for X-Frame-Options header
Checks for Referrer-Policy header
Checks for Permissions-Policy header
Checks for X-Powered-By and Server version disclosure
Provides specific implementation code for missing headers

evals.prompt: My WordPress site isn't ranking well on Google. Can you help with SEO?
evals.expected_output: Should recognize this is an SEO question, not a security question. Should recommend the market-seo-audit skill instead. May briefly mention that security issues (like missing HTTPS or slow performance from security plugins) can affect SEO, but should not conduct a security audit.
assertions: Recognizes this is an SEO question, not a security question
Recommends or defers to the market-seo-audit skill
Does not conduct a security audit for an SEO question
